revision.text:
[[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11519732]] Wikidata name: end stage kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|terminal-phase kofun|ja|末期古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terminal-phase kofun|qq=}} (末期古墳 ) '''Matsuki Kofun''' (末期古墳) refers to [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) constructed between the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}} and the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century}} in the northern part of the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region}} and the Ishikari lowland area of [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. These kofun are characterized by their circular shape, relatively low mounds, and surrounding moats. Also known as '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun''', they are sometimes referred to as Ezo-style tombs, as they are believed to have been constructed by the people called {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}}. Matsuki Kofun differs conceptually from {{ill|Shūmatsuki Kofun|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmatsuki Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}. == Discovery and Naming of Matsuki Kofun == [[File:Kumadoukohungun-1.JPG|right|250px|thumb|The Kumadō Kofun Cluster in Hanamaki, Iwate Prefecture. Excavations revealed Wadōkaichin coins, suggesting construction in the 8th century.]] In {{ill|1931|en|1931|WD=Q18782|lt=1931}}, the {{ill|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|en|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11550632|lt=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster}} was discovered in [[Sapporo District]], Ebetsu Town, Hokkaidō (now [[Ebetsu City]]). In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}}, based on the excavation results of the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster and the Mogaru Kofun Cluster (Kashiwagi East Site) in [[Chitose District]], Eniwa Village (now [[Eniwa City]]), [[Gorō Goto|Gotō Morikazu]] proposed the term '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun'''{{Cite book |author=Yagi Mitsunori |title=東北北部の終末期古墳群 |publisher=Iwate Archaeological Society |year=1996 |pages=62}}. This term was meant to distinguish these mounded tombs from the kofun built across Honshu during the [[Kofun period]]. However, whether these tombs qualify as kofun has been debated, with some researchers classifying them as mound tombs and others proposing the term Matsuki Kofun in the {{ill|1960s|en|1960s|WD=Q35724|lt=1960s}}. Subsequently, tombs similar to Hokkaidō-style Kofun were discovered in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, leading to broader use of the term Matsuki Kofun. Even today, it is unclear whether Matsuki Kofun are kofun, and the term Ezo-style tombs has also been proposed. == Distribution and Construction Period == Matsuki Kofun are distributed across {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, and northern {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}Ōigawa Basin and lower Kitakami River Basin, as well as the Ishikari lowland area in central Hokkaidō. Additionally, uninvestigated clusters of small circular tombs in the [[Shōnai Plain]] of {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} may also qualify as Matsuki Kofun. Their construction dates range from the early 7th century to the 10th century. Construction began in the upper and middle reaches of the {{ill|Kitakami River|en|Kitakami River|WD=Q1348297|lt=Kitakami River}} and the {{ill|Mabechi River|en|Mabechi River|WD=Q11668006|lt=Mabechi River}} Basin in the early 7th century, spreading to northern Tōhoku and the Ishikari lowland in Hokkaidō between the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century}} and {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}. Volcanic ash from the [[Towada Caldera]] eruption in {{ill|915|en|915|WD=Q24031|lt=915}} (Enchō 15) and ash attributed to a 10th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Baekdu|Mount Baekdu]] indicate that some Matsuki Kofun were constructed until the early 10th centuryKoyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005, p. 227. Many Matsuki Kofun were built as clustered Kofuns, similar to [[gunshūfun]]. For example, the [[Akōbō Kofun Cluster]] in Aomori Prefecture consists of over 100 Matsuki Kofun. Long-term construction is evident in some clusters, such as the [[Tankōdaira Kofun Cluster]], built from the late 7th to the late 9th century, and the Akōbō Kofun Cluster, built over nearly 300 years. Before Matsuki Kofun construction began, Kofuns associated with the [[Zoku-Jōmon culture]] were constructed in northern Tōhoku and Hokkaidō. These earlier mounds lacked surrounding moats and were simple in form, with small graves and flexed burials. In contrast, Matsuki Kofun featured larger mounds, moats, and burials with extended bodies. While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Few intact mounds remain, but well-preserved examples suggest mounds were about 1 meter tall, rarely exceeding 2 meters. == Burial Structures and Grave Goods == Burial structures include direct interments in wooden coffins placed in [[soil pits]] and stone chambers derived from {{ill|Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|zh|橫穴式石室|ko|굴식_돌방무덤}}. The direct interment type is widespread and spans the entire Matsuki Kofun period. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Stone chambers, present only in the Kitakami River Basin, evolved from kofun-style yokoana-shiki sekishitsu but were used differently, with bodies likely interred from above. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Grave goods changed after the early 8th century, shifting from horse gear influenced by the eastern provinces to items like [[warabite-tō]] and belt fittings indicating [[ritsuryō system]] Ranks. Coins like {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|WD=Q3565040|lt=Wadōkaichin}} were also found, possibly linked to trade or tributeKumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2004, p. 100. == Characteristics of Matsuki Kofun == Matsuki Kofun were built after the decline of traditional kofun construction, starting in the 7th century and continuing into the early 10th century. Unlike traditional kofun, they were constructed in regions without earlier kofun, primarily by Ezo communities. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

Also known as '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun''', they are sometimes referred to as Ezo-style tombs, as they are believed to have been constructed by the people called {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}}. Matsuki Kofun differs conceptually from {{ill|Shūmatsuki Kofun|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmatsuki Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}. == Discovery and Naming of Matsuki Kofun == [[File:Kumadoukohungun-1.JPG|right|250px|thumb|The Kumadō Kofun Cluster in Hanamaki, Iwate Prefecture. Excavations revealed Wadōkaichin coins, suggesting construction in the 8th century.]] In {{ill|1931|en|1931|WD=Q18782|lt=1931}}, the {{ill|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|en|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11550632|lt=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster}} was discovered in [[Sapporo District]], Ebetsu Town, Hokkaidō (now [[Ebetsu City]]). In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}}, based on the excavation results of the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster and the Mogaru Kofun Cluster (Kashiwagi East Site) in [[Chitose District]], Eniwa Village (now [[Eniwa City]]), [[Gorō Goto|Gotō Morikazu]] proposed the term '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun'''{{Cite book |author=Yagi Mitsunori |title=東北北部の終末期古墳群 |publisher=Iwate Archaeological Society |year=1996 |pages=62}}. This term was meant to distinguish these mounded tombs from the kofun built across Honshu during the [[Kofun period]]. However, whether these tombs qualify as kofun has been debated, with some researchers classifying them as mound tombs and others proposing the term Matsuki Kofun in the {{ill|1960s|en|1960s|WD=Q35724|lt=1960s}}. Subsequently, tombs similar to Hokkaidō-style Kofun were discovered in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, leading to broader use of the term Matsuki Kofun. Even today, it is unclear whether Matsuki Kofun are kofun, and the term Ezo-style tombs has also been proposed. == Distribution and Construction Period == Matsuki Kofun are distributed across {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, and northern {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}Ōigawa Basin and lower Kitakami River Basin, as well as the Ishikari lowland area in central Hokkaidō. Additionally, uninvestigated clusters of small circular tombs in the [[Shōnai Plain]] of {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} may also qualify as Matsuki Kofun. Their construction dates range from the early 7th century to the 10th century. Construction began in the upper and middle reaches of the {{ill|Kitakami River|en|Kitakami River|WD=Q1348297|lt=Kitakami River}} and the {{ill|Mabechi River|en|Mabechi River|WD=Q11668006|lt=Mabechi River}} Basin in the early 7th century, spreading to northern Tōhoku and the Ishikari lowland in Hokkaidō between the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century}} and {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}. Volcanic ash from the [[Towada Caldera]] eruption in {{ill|915|en|915|WD=Q24031|lt=915}} (Enchō 15) and ash attributed to a 10th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Baekdu|Mount Baekdu]] indicate that some Matsuki Kofun were constructed until the early 10th centuryKoyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005, p. 227. Many Matsuki Kofun were built as clustered Kofuns, similar to [[gunshūfun]]. For example, the [[Akōbō Kofun Cluster]] in Aomori Prefecture consists of over 100 Matsuki Kofun. Long-term construction is evident in some clusters, such as the [[Tankōdaira Kofun Cluster]], built from the late 7th to the late 9th century, and the Akōbō Kofun Cluster, built over nearly 300 years. Before Matsuki Kofun construction began, Kofuns associated with the [[Zoku-Jōmon culture]] were constructed in northern Tōhoku and Hokkaidō. These earlier mounds lacked surrounding moats and were simple in form, with small graves and flexed burials. In contrast, Matsuki Kofun featured larger mounds, moats, and burials with extended bodies. While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Few intact mounds remain, but well-preserved examples suggest mounds were about 1 meter tall, rarely exceeding 2 meters. == Burial Structures and Grave Goods == Burial structures include direct interments in wooden coffins placed in [[soil pits]] and stone chambers derived from {{ill|Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|zh|橫穴式石室|ko|굴식_돌방무덤}}. The direct interment type is widespread and spans the entire Matsuki Kofun period. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Stone chambers, present only in the Kitakami River Basin, evolved from kofun-style yokoana-shiki sekishitsu but were used differently, with bodies likely interred from above. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Grave goods changed after the early 8th century, shifting from horse gear influenced by the eastern provinces to items like [[warabite-tō]] and belt fittings indicating [[ritsuryō system]] Ranks. Coins like {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|WD=Q3565040|lt=Wadōkaichin}} were also found, possibly linked to trade or tributeKumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2004, p. 100. == Characteristics of Matsuki Kofun == Matsuki Kofun were built after the decline of traditional kofun construction, starting in the 7th century and continuing into the early 10th century. Unlike traditional kofun, they were constructed in regions without earlier kofun, primarily by Ezo communities. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

These kofun are characterized by their circular shape, relatively low mounds, and surrounding moats. Also known as '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun''', they are sometimes referred to as Ezo-style tombs, as they are believed to have been constructed by the people called {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}}. Matsuki Kofun differs conceptually from {{ill|Shūmatsuki Kofun|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmatsuki Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}. == Discovery and Naming of Matsuki Kofun == [[File:Kumadoukohungun-1.JPG|right|250px|thumb|The Kumadō Kofun Cluster in Hanamaki, Iwate Prefecture. Excavations revealed Wadōkaichin coins, suggesting construction in the 8th century.]] In {{ill|1931|en|1931|WD=Q18782|lt=1931}}, the {{ill|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|en|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11550632|lt=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster}} was discovered in [[Sapporo District]], Ebetsu Town, Hokkaidō (now [[Ebetsu City]]). However, whether these tombs qualify as kofun has been debated, with some researchers classifying them as mound tombs and others proposing the term Matsuki Kofun in the {{ill|1960s|en|1960s|WD=Q35724|lt=1960s}}. Subsequently, tombs similar to Hokkaidō-style Kofun were discovered in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, leading to broader use of the term Matsuki Kofun. Even today, it is unclear whether Matsuki Kofun are kofun, and the term Ezo-style tombs has also been proposed. == Distribution and Construction Period == Matsuki Kofun are distributed across {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, and northern {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}Ōigawa Basin and lower Kitakami River Basin, as well as the Ishikari lowland area in central Hokkaidō. Additionally, uninvestigated clusters of small circular tombs in the [[Shōnai Plain]] of {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} may also qualify as Matsuki Kofun. Their construction dates range from the early 7th century to the 10th century. Construction began in the upper and middle reaches of the {{ill|Kitakami River|en|Kitakami River|WD=Q1348297|lt=Kitakami River}} and the {{ill|Mabechi River|en|Mabechi River|WD=Q11668006|lt=Mabechi River}} Basin in the early 7th century, spreading to northern Tōhoku and the Ishikari lowland in Hokkaidō between the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century}} and {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century}}. Volcanic ash from the [[Towada Caldera]] eruption in {{ill|915|en|915|WD=Q24031|lt=915}} (Enchō 15) and ash attributed to a 10th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Baekdu|Mount Baekdu]] indicate that some Matsuki Kofun were constructed until the early 10th centuryKoyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005, p. 227. These earlier mounds lacked surrounding moats and were simple in form, with small graves and flexed burials. In contrast, Matsuki Kofun featured larger mounds, moats, and burials with extended bodies. While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Few intact mounds remain, but well-preserved examples suggest mounds were about 1 meter tall, rarely exceeding 2 meters. == Burial Structures and Grave Goods == Burial structures include direct interments in wooden coffins placed in [[soil pits]] and stone chambers derived from {{ill|Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|zh|橫穴式石室|ko|굴식_돌방무덤}}. The direct interment type is widespread and spans the entire Matsuki Kofun period. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Stone chambers, present only in the Kitakami River Basin, evolved from kofun-style yokoana-shiki sekishitsu but were used differently, with bodies likely interred from above. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Grave goods changed after the early 8th century, shifting from horse gear influenced by the eastern provinces to items like [[warabite-tō]] and belt fittings indicating [[ritsuryō system]] Ranks. Coins like {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|WD=Q3565040|lt=Wadōkaichin|1=Wadōkaichin}} were also found, possibly linked to trade or tributeKumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2004, p. 100. == Characteristics of Matsuki Kofun == Matsuki Kofun were built after the decline of traditional kofun construction, starting in the 7th century and continuing into the early 10th century. Unlike traditional kofun, they were constructed in regions without earlier kofun, primarily by Ezo communities. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture|1=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] {{ill|terminal-phase kofun|ja|末期古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terminal-phase kofun|qq=}} (末期古墳 ) '''Matsuki Kofun''' (末期古墳) refers to [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) constructed between the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} and the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century|1=10th century}} in the northern part of the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} and the Ishikari lowland area of [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. These kofun are characterized by their circular shape, relatively low mounds, and surrounding moats. Also known as '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun''', they are sometimes referred to as Ezo-style tombs, as they are believed to have been constructed by the people called {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}}. Matsuki Kofun differs conceptually from {{ill|Shūmatsuki Kofun|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmatsuki Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}. == Discovery and Naming of Matsuki Kofun == [[File:Kumadoukohungun-1.JPG|right|250px|thumb|The Kumadō Kofun Cluster in Hanamaki, Iwate Prefecture. Excavations revealed Wadōkaichin coins, suggesting construction in the 8th century.]] In {{ill|1931|en|1931|WD=Q18782|lt=1931|1=1931}}, the {{ill|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|en|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11550632|lt=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|1=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster}} was discovered in [[Sapporo District]], Ebetsu Town, Hokkaidō (now [[Ebetsu City]]). In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}}, based on the excavation results of the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster and the Mogaru Kofun Cluster (Kashiwagi East Site) in [[Chitose District]], Eniwa Village (now [[Eniwa City]]), [[Gorō Goto|Gotō Morikazu]] proposed the term '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun'''{{Cite book |author=Yagi Mitsunori |title=東北北部の終末期古墳群 |publisher=Iwate Archaeological Society |year=1996 |pages=62}}. This term was meant to distinguish these mounded tombs from the kofun built across Honshu during the [[Kofun period]]. However, whether these tombs qualify as kofun has been debated, with some researchers classifying them as mound tombs and others proposing the term Matsuki Kofun in the {{ill|1960s|en|1960s|WD=Q35724|lt=1960s|1=1960s}}. Subsequently, tombs similar to Hokkaidō-style Kofun were discovered in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, leading to broader use of the term Matsuki Kofun. Even today, it is unclear whether Matsuki Kofun are kofun, and the term Ezo-style tombs has also been proposed. == Distribution and Construction Period == Matsuki Kofun are distributed across {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, and northern {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}Ōigawa Basin and lower Kitakami River Basin, as well as the Ishikari lowland area in central Hokkaidō. Additionally, uninvestigated clusters of small circular tombs in the [[Shōnai Plain]] of {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} may also qualify as Matsuki Kofun. Their construction dates range from the early 7th century to the 10th century. Construction began in the upper and middle reaches of the {{ill|Kitakami River|en|Kitakami River|WD=Q1348297|lt=Kitakami River|1=Kitakami River}} and the {{ill|Mabechi River|en|Mabechi River|WD=Q11668006|lt=Mabechi River|1=Mabechi River}} Basin in the early 7th century, spreading to northern Tōhoku and the Ishikari lowland in Hokkaidō between the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century|1=8th century}} and {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}}. Volcanic ash from the [[Towada Caldera]] eruption in {{ill|915|en|915|WD=Q24031|lt=915|1=915}} (Enchō 15) and ash attributed to a 10th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Baekdu|Mount Baekdu]] indicate that some Matsuki Kofun were constructed until the early 10th centuryKoyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005, p. 227. Many Matsuki Kofun were built as clustered Kofuns, similar to [[gunshūfun]]. For example, the [[Akōbō Kofun Cluster]] in Aomori Prefecture consists of over 100 Matsuki Kofun. Long-term construction is evident in some clusters, such as the [[Tankōdaira Kofun Cluster]], built from the late 7th to the late 9th century, and the Akōbō Kofun Cluster, built over nearly 300 years. Before Matsuki Kofun construction began, Kofuns associated with the [[Zoku-Jōmon culture]] were constructed in northern Tōhoku and Hokkaidō. These earlier mounds lacked surrounding moats and were simple in form, with small graves and flexed burials. In contrast, Matsuki Kofun featured larger mounds, moats, and burials with extended bodies. While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Few intact mounds remain, but well-preserved examples suggest mounds were about 1 meter tall, rarely exceeding 2 meters. == Burial Structures and Grave Goods == Burial structures include direct interments in wooden coffins placed in [[soil pits]] and stone chambers derived from {{ill|Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|zh|橫穴式石室|ko|굴식_돌방무덤}}. The direct interment type is widespread and spans the entire Matsuki Kofun period. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Stone chambers, present only in the Kitakami River Basin, evolved from kofun-style yokoana-shiki sekishitsu but were used differently, with bodies likely interred from above. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Grave goods changed after the early 8th century, shifting from horse gear influenced by the eastern provinces to items like [[warabite-tō]] and belt fittings indicating [[ritsuryō system]] Ranks. Coins like {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|WD=Q3565040|lt=Wadōkaichin|1=Wadōkaichin}} were also found, possibly linked to trade or tributeKumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2004, p. 100. == Characteristics of Matsuki Kofun == Matsuki Kofun were built after the decline of traditional kofun construction, starting in the 7th century and continuing into the early 10th century. Unlike traditional kofun, they were constructed in regions without earlier kofun, primarily by Ezo communities. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture|1=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpōkōenfun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture|1=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.text:
[[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] [[ja:末期古墳]] {{ill|terminal-phase kofun|ja|末期古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terminal-phase kofun|qq=}} (末期古墳 ) '''Matsuki Kofun''' (末期古墳) refers to [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns) constructed between the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} and the {{ill|10th century|en|10th century|WD=Q8052|lt=10th century|1=10th century}} in the northern part of the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} and the Ishikari lowland area of [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. These kofun are characterized by their circular shape, relatively low mounds, and surrounding moats. Also known as '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun''', they are sometimes referred to as Ezo-style tombs, as they are believed to have been constructed by the people called {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}}. Matsuki Kofun differs conceptually from {{ill|Shūmatsuki Kofun|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmatsuki Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}. == Discovery and Naming of Matsuki Kofun == [[File:Kumadoukohungun-1.JPG|right|250px|thumb|The Kumadō Kofun Cluster in Hanamaki, Iwate Prefecture. Excavations revealed Wadōkaichin coins, suggesting construction in the 8th century.]] In {{ill|1931|en|1931|WD=Q18782|lt=1931|1=1931}}, the {{ill|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|en|Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11550632|lt=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster|1=Ebetsu Kofun Cluster}} was discovered in [[Sapporo District]], Ebetsu Town, Hokkaidō (now [[Ebetsu City]]). In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}}, based on the excavation results of the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster and the Mogaru Kofun Cluster (Kashiwagi East Site) in [[Chitose District]], Eniwa Village (now [[Eniwa City]]), [[Gorō Goto|Gotō Morikazu]] proposed the term '''Hokkaidō-style Kofun'''{{Cite book |author=Yagi Mitsunori |title=東北北部の終末期古墳群 |publisher=Iwate Archaeological Society |year=1996 |pages=62}}. This term was meant to distinguish these mounded tombs from the kofun built across Honshu during the [[Kofun period]]. However, whether these tombs qualify as kofun has been debated, with some researchers classifying them as mound tombs and others proposing the term Matsuki Kofun in the {{ill|1960s|en|1960s|WD=Q35724|lt=1960s|1=1960s}}. Subsequently, tombs similar to Hokkaidō-style Kofun were discovered in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, leading to broader use of the term Matsuki Kofun. Even today, it is unclear whether Matsuki Kofun are kofun, and the term Ezo-style tombs has also been proposed. == Distribution and Construction Period == Matsuki Kofun are distributed across {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, and northern {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}Ōigawa Basin and lower Kitakami River Basin, as well as the Ishikari lowland area in central Hokkaidō. Additionally, uninvestigated clusters of small circular tombs in the [[Shōnai Plain]] of {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} may also qualify as Matsuki Kofun. Their construction dates range from the early 7th century to the 10th century. Construction began in the upper and middle reaches of the {{ill|Kitakami River|en|Kitakami River|WD=Q1348297|lt=Kitakami River|1=Kitakami River}} and the {{ill|Mabechi River|en|Mabechi River|WD=Q11668006|lt=Mabechi River|1=Mabechi River}} Basin in the early 7th century, spreading to northern Tōhoku and the Ishikari lowland in Hokkaidō between the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century|1=8th century}} and {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|WD=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}}. Volcanic ash from the [[Towada Caldera]] eruption in {{ill|915|en|915|WD=Q24031|lt=915|1=915}} (Enchō 15) and ash attributed to a 10th-century eruption of [[:en:Mount Baekdu|Mount Baekdu]] indicate that some Matsuki Kofun were constructed until the early 10th centuryKoyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpō-kōen-fun Research Association, 2005, p. 227. Many Matsuki Kofun were built as clustered Kofuns, similar to [[gunshūfun]]. For example, the [[Akōbō Kofun Cluster]] in Aomori Prefecture consists of over 100 Matsuki Kofun. Long-term construction is evident in some clusters, such as the [[Tankōdaira Kofun Cluster]], built from the late 7th to the late 9th century, and the Akōbō Kofun Cluster, built over nearly 300 years. Before Matsuki Kofun construction began, Kofuns associated with the [[Zoku-Jōmon culture]] were constructed in northern Tōhoku and Hokkaidō. These earlier mounds lacked surrounding moats and were simple in form, with small graves and flexed burials. In contrast, Matsuki Kofun featured larger mounds, moats, and burials with extended bodies. While Hokkaidō continued building Zoku-Jōmon-style Kofuns during the Matsuki Kofun period, the introduction of kofun suggests cultural influence from Honshu. == Features and Scale == Matsuki Kofun are predominantly circular, with occasional elliptical or square shapes. They typically feature surrounding moats. Mounds range from 5 to 10 meters in diameter, with the largest measuring about 17 meters. Few intact mounds remain, but well-preserved examples suggest mounds were about 1 meter tall, rarely exceeding 2 meters. == Burial Structures and Grave Goods == Burial structures include direct interments in wooden coffins placed in [[soil pits]] and stone chambers derived from {{ill|Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|zh|橫穴式石室|ko|굴식_돌방무덤}}. The direct interment type is widespread and spans the entire Matsuki Kofun period. Initially, burial chambers were built on the ground and then covered with mounds, but after the late 8th century, mounds were constructed first, and chambers were excavated into them. Stone chambers, present only in the Kitakami River Basin, evolved from kofun-style yokoana-shiki sekishitsu but were used differently, with bodies likely interred from above. Grave goods include weapons such as swords, decorative items like beads, and practical items like horse gear, belt fittings, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}. These items were modest and lacked the opulence seen in earlier kofun, suggesting a more egalitarian societyFujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," in 古墳時代の政治構造, Hirose Kazuo et al., Aoki Shoten, 2004, p. 301. Grave goods changed after the early 8th century, shifting from horse gear influenced by the eastern provinces to items like [[warabite-tō]] and belt fittings indicating [[ritsuryō system]] Ranks. Coins like {{ill|Wadōkaichin|en|Wadōkaichin|WD=Q3565040|lt=Wadōkaichin|1=Wadōkaichin}} were also found, possibly linked to trade or tributeKumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2004, p. 100. == Characteristics of Matsuki Kofun == Matsuki Kofun were built after the decline of traditional kofun construction, starting in the 7th century and continuing into the early 10th century. Unlike traditional kofun, they were constructed in regions without earlier kofun, primarily by Ezo communities. Their egalitarian nature contrasts sharply with the hierarchical structure of traditional kofun society. Although distinct from Zoku-Jōmon Kofuns, Matsuki Kofun reflect interaction with {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} culture. Features such as surrounding moats and stone chambers indicate influence from Honshu, while burial goods show connections to the [[ritsuryō state]]. Sites like the Ebetsu Kofun Cluster in Hokkaidō, the Akōbō and Tankōdaira Kofun Clusters in Aomori, and the [[Ezuriko Kofun Cluster|Etsuriko Kofun Cluster]] in Iwate are designated [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] for their importance in understanding Ezo society and its ties to the Yamato state. == References ==
== Further Reading == * Yagi Mitsunori, "東北北部の終末期古墳群," 岩手考古学第8号, 1996 * Kumagai Kimio, "日本史リブレット 蝦夷の地と古代国家," 山川出版社, 2004 * Fujisawa Atsushi, "倭の古墳と東北北部の末期古墳," 広瀬和雄他, 青木書店, 2004, ISBN 4-250-20410-3 * Suzuki Shinobu, "北海道式古墳の実像," 北海道新聞社, 2004 * Koyachi Hajime, "末期古墳の展開," Tōhoku-Kantō Zenpō-kōen-fun Research Association, 2005 * Hirose Kazuo, "前方後円墳の世界," 岩波書店, 2010, ISBN 978-4-00-431264-2 == See Also == * {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Zoku-Jōmon period|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} * {{ill|Satsumon culture|en|Satsumon culture|WD=Q2614019|lt=Satsumon culture|1=Satsumon culture}} * [[Kofun]] * {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} * [[Ezo campaigns]] == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/mshomei/pareo/33kai-shiryou/kohun/kohun.htm |title=「末期古墳」と「倭」の古墳 |date=20160304123933}} * [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/rekishi/rekisi/data/jyomon_7.html 【縄文～古墳時代】岩手の終末期古墳] - [http://www.bunka.pref.iwate.jp/ Iwate Culture Encyclopedia] (Iwate Prefecture) {{translated page|ja|末期古墳|version=92057255|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:末期古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11519732}} [[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:Ezo]] [[Category:History of Hokkaido]] [[Category:History of the Tōhoku region]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Terminal-phase kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
